questions.question: Which change is most likely to be backward compatible by default?
options: Adding an optional response field while preserving existing semantics.
Removing a field that some clients may still read.
Changing the meaning of an existing enum value.
Reordering results without documenting the new behavior.
questions.explanation: Additive changes are usually safer than removals or semantic changes.

questions.question: Why is API versioning an operational concern rather than just a URL naming choice?
options: Because old clients, telemetry, migrations, and deprecation windows all affect system behavior over time.
Because version numbers must always increase every week.
Because only internal services ever need compatibility.
Because once `/v2` exists, `/v1` can always be turned off immediately.
questions.explanation: Versioning changes support load, rollout strategy, monitoring, and client coordination.

questions.question: What is the main risk of treating a semantic change as an additive one?
options: Clients may continue compiling while silently behaving incorrectly.
The API becomes impossible to document.
The service can no longer paginate results.
The system automatically switches to eventual consistency.
questions.explanation: Semantic drift is especially dangerous because shape-only validation may miss it.

questions.question: Which metric best tells you whether a deprecation is actually safe to enforce?
options: The number of new feature ideas in the roadmap.
Request volume and error rate broken down by API version and client type.
The amount of time spent writing SDK examples.
The total number of fields in the latest schema.
questions.explanation: You need to see who is still using older versions and whether they are healthy.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping a translation layer for old clients?
options: Smoother migrations, but more code paths and compatibility testing to maintain.
Zero maintenance cost and instant client upgrades.
Guaranteed elimination of breaking changes forever.
The ability to avoid any version telemetry.
questions.explanation: Translation layers buy compatibility at the cost of complexity and test surface.

questions.question: When is an explicit new version usually warranted?
options: When the change materially breaks semantics or contract expectations for supported clients.
When adding any optional field to a response payload.
Whenever the engineering team wants a fresh documentation page.
Only when changing the underlying storage engine.
questions.explanation: Hard version boundaries are most justified for real incompatibility, not cosmetic changes.

questions.question: What failure pattern often causes version sprawl?
options: Supporting new versions before defining retirement rules and telemetry for old ones.
Using typed clients for internal services.
Rejecting all breaking changes permanently.
Monitoring latency by version.
questions.explanation: Without support windows and observability, old versions linger indefinitely.

questions.question: If partner clients upgrade slowly for a year, what is the strongest response?
options: Shut off the old version on schedule and hope for the best.
Maintain clear support windows, version telemetry, communication, and a migration plan that matches client reality.
Stop making any changes to the API forever.
Replace the entire protocol stack with events.
questions.explanation: Long-tail client upgrades require operational planning, not just a new endpoint name.
